items.title: Decompile and review APK
items.description: Use jadx or apktool to inspect source, manifest, and hardcoded secrets.
items.how_to_find:
Pull and decompile the APK to review code/resources:

```
adb shell pm path <pkg> && adb pull <path> app.apk
apktool d app.apk -o out/           # resources + smali
jadx-gui app.apk                    # decompiled Java
```

Automated baseline: `mobsf` (upload the APK).
items.how_to_fix: Fix issues surfaced (secrets, insecure storage, exported components). Add code shrinking/obfuscation (R8/ProGuard), strip debug artifacts, and keep sensitive logic server-side. Re-scan with MobSF after remediation.

items.title: AndroidManifest exported components
items.description: Flag exported activities, services, receivers, and providers without proper permissions.
items.how_to_find:
List exported activities/services/receivers/providers reachable by other apps:

```
aapt dump xmltree app.apk AndroidManifest.xml | grep -iE 'exported|permission'
# review out/AndroidManifest.xml for android:exported="true" without a permission
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `android:exported="false"` for components not meant to be public, protect required ones with signature-level permissions + input validation, and avoid implicit intents for sensitive actions. Re-audit the manifest.

items.title: Hardcoded API keys and secrets
items.description: Search decompiled code and resources for keys, tokens, and credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Grep decompiled code/resources for secrets:

```
grep -REn 'api[_-]?key|secret|password|AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}|-----BEGIN' out/
# scan native libs too: strings out/lib/*/*.so | grep -i key
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove secrets from the client (any shipped secret is extractable); move them server-side behind an authenticated API, use short-lived tokens, and rotate exposed keys. Re-scan to confirm none remain.

items.title: Insecure local storage
items.description: Check SharedPreferences, SQLite, and external storage for sensitive data.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect app data storage for plaintext sensitive data:

```
adb shell run-as <pkg> ls -R /data/data/<pkg>/
# review shared_prefs/, databases/, files/ for tokens/PII
```
items.how_to_fix: Store secrets in Android Keystore-backed encryption (Jetpack Security `EncryptedSharedPreferences`/SQLCipher), never in plaintext prefs/DB, and minimize what's persisted. Re-inspect storage.

items.title: Backup allowed
items.description: Verify android:allowBackup is false for apps handling sensitive data.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether app data can be extracted via backup:

```
grep -i 'allowBackup' out/AndroidManifest.xml
adb backup -f data.ab <pkg>    # succeeds if backups allowed
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `android:allowBackup="false"` (or define strict `fullBackupContent`/`dataExtractionRules` excluding sensitive data), and rely on Keystore encryption. Re-test that backup excludes sensitive data.

items.title: Debuggable flag
items.description: Ensure release builds are not debuggable.
items.how_to_find:
Check the debuggable flag (allows runtime debugging/data access):

```
aapt dump badging app.apk | grep -i debuggable
grep -i 'android:debuggable' out/AndroidManifest.xml
```
items.how_to_fix: Ensure release builds set `android:debuggable="false"` (default for release), fail CI on debuggable release APKs, and strip debug logging. Re-verify the release build.

items.title: Certificate pinning bypass
items.description: Test with Frida/objection if traffic can be intercepted via Burp.
items.how_to_find:
Attempt to intercept TLS and bypass pinning to inspect traffic:

```
frida -U -f <pkg> -l frida-multiple-unpinning.js
objection -g <pkg> explore   # android sslpinning disable
```

If traffic becomes readable through Burp, pinning is bypassable.
items.how_to_fix: Implement robust pinning (OkHttp `CertificatePinner` / Network Security Config), combine with tamper/hook detection, and never rely on pinning alone. Enforce server-side authz. Note: determined attackers can bypass; keep secrets server-side.

items.title: Root detection bypass
items.description: Attempt to run app on rooted device or emulator with hooks.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether root detection can be defeated:

```
frida -U -f <pkg> -l root-bypass.js
objection -g <pkg> explore   # android root disable
```
items.how_to_fix: Use layered, obfuscated root checks (e.g. Play Integrity API attestation server-side) rather than a single client check, and make security decisions server-side. Re-test bypass resistance.

items.title: Deep link hijacking
items.description: Test custom URL schemes and App Links for takeover by malicious apps.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate and fuzz deep links / app links:

```
grep -A5 'intent-filter' out/AndroidManifest.xml
adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d "myapp://path?param=..." <pkg>
```
items.how_to_fix: Use Android App Links with verified `assetlinks.json` (autoVerify), validate all deep-link parameters, require auth for sensitive actions, and avoid trusting link data. Re-test hijacking/param injection.

items.title: IPC abuse
items.description: Use drozer to test content providers and inter-process communication.
items.how_to_find:
Invoke exported components / providers directly:

```
adb shell am start -n <pkg>/<activity> --es key value
adb shell content query --uri content://<authority>/<path>
```
items.how_to_fix: Unexport internal components, enforce permissions + input validation on exposed ones, and use signature permissions for internal IPC. Re-test direct invocation is refused.

items.title: Runtime instrumentation
items.description: Hook crypto, auth, and SSL routines with Frida.
items.how_to_find:
Hook methods at runtime to observe/alter behavior:

```
frida-trace -U -i "*crypto*" -f <pkg>
objection -g <pkg> explore
```
items.how_to_fix: Add anti-hooking/anti-Frida + integrity detection (defense-in-depth), obfuscate, and keep trust decisions server-side. Re-test instrumentation resistance.

items.title: Backend API replay
items.description: Capture and replay mobile API calls in Burp; test IDOR and auth.
items.how_to_find:
Capture app API calls and replay/tamper them:

```
# proxy via Burp (with pinning bypass), then replay/modify requests
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce server-side authz on every request, use short-lived tokens + nonces/anti-replay, validate all input server-side, and don't trust client-side checks. Re-test replay/tamper is rejected.

items.title: Binary analysis
items.description: Inspect IPA with class-dump, Hopper, or Ghidra for secrets and logic.
items.how_to_find:
Analyze the decrypted IPA/binary:

```
unzip app.ipa -d ipa/ && ls ipa/Payload/*.app
otool -l ipa/Payload/*.app/<bin> | grep -iE 'encryption|PIE|stack'
strings -a <bin> | grep -iE 'http|key|secret'
```

Automated: MobSF (upload IPA).
items.how_to_fix: Enable PIE/stack canaries/ARC (default in modern Xcode), remove secrets/debug strings, and keep sensitive logic server-side. Re-scan with MobSF.

items.title: Info.plist review
items.description: Check ATS settings, URL schemes, and sensitive plist keys.
items.how_to_find:
Review Info.plist for insecure settings (ATS exceptions, URL schemes, permissions):

```
plutil -p ipa/Payload/*.app/Info.plist | grep -iE 'ATS|AppTransport|URLScheme|NSAllowsArbitrary'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove ATS exceptions (`NSAllowsArbitraryLoads`), declare only needed URL schemes/permissions with usage strings, and avoid insecure flags. Re-review the plist.

items.title: Hardcoded secrets in bundle
items.description: Search strings and plists for API keys and credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Search the app bundle for secrets:

```
grep -REn 'api[_-]?key|secret|password|-----BEGIN' ipa/Payload/*.app/
strings -a <bin> | grep -iE 'key|token|secret'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove all shipped secrets (extractable), move them server-side, use short-lived tokens, and rotate exposed values. Re-scan the bundle.

items.title: Jailbreak detection
items.description: Identify and test bypass of jailbreak/root checks.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether jailbreak detection can be bypassed:

```
objection -g <bundle-id> explore   # ios jailbreak disable
frida -U -f <bundle-id> -l ios-jb-bypass.js
```
items.how_to_fix: Use layered/obfuscated JB checks plus DeviceCheck/App Attest attestation validated server-side, and make trust decisions server-side. Re-test bypass resistance.

items.title: Keychain accessibility
items.description: Dump keychain with objection; verify kSecAttrAccessible classes.
items.how_to_find:
Dump Keychain items and check protection classes:

```
objection -g <bundle-id> explore   # ios keychain dump
```

Items with `Always`/`AfterFirstUnlock` accessibility for sensitive data are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Store secrets in Keychain with `WhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly` (+ `.biometryCurrentSet`/access control), avoid `Always`, and don't sync sensitive items to iCloud. Re-dump to verify protection classes.

items.title: SSL pinning bypass
items.description: Use Frida/objection ios sslpinning disable and proxy traffic.
items.how_to_find:
Attempt to bypass pinning and intercept:

```
objection -g <bundle-id> explore   # ios sslpinning disable
frida -U -f <bundle-id> -l ios-ssl-bypass.js
```
items.how_to_fix: Implement strong pinning (e.g. TrustKit) with hook/tamper detection, and enforce server-side authz. Keep secrets server-side (pinning is bypassable on jailbroken devices). Re-test.

items.title: Universal link hijacking
items.description: Test associated domains and URL scheme handlers.
items.how_to_find:
Test universal links / custom URL schemes for hijacking or unvalidated params:

```
xcrun simctl openurl booted "myapp://path?x=..."
# review apple-app-site-association
```
items.how_to_fix: Use Universal Links with a correct `apple-app-site-association`, avoid sensitive custom URL schemes (any app can claim them), validate all parameters, and require auth for sensitive actions. Re-test.

items.title: Sensitive data in logs
items.description: Monitor device logs during auth and payment flows.
items.how_to_find:
Observe device logs while exercising the app:

```
idevicesyslog | grep -iE 'token|password|card|ssn'
# or Console.app filtered by the app
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove sensitive data from logs, disable verbose logging in release, and use os_log with private redaction. Re-check logs are clean.

items.title: Backend API security
items.description: Apply web/API testing to endpoints the app calls.
items.how_to_find:
Proxy and test the app's API (authz, IDOR, replay):

```
# Burp with pinning bypass; tamper IDs/params, replay requests
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce server-side authz on every endpoint/object, validate input, use short-lived tokens + anti-replay, and don't trust the client. Re-test.

items.title: Clipboard and screenshot leaks
items.description: Verify sensitive fields are not copied or captured.
items.how_to_find:
Check for sensitive data on the pasteboard and in the app-switcher snapshot:

```
objection -g <bundle-id> explore   # ios pasteboard monitor
# background the app on a sensitive screen and view the snapshot
```
items.how_to_fix: Avoid copying secrets to the general pasteboard (use expiring/local items), and mask sensitive screens on backgrounding (blur/placeholder view). Re-test clipboard + snapshot.

sections.title: Data Storage & Privacy — Insecure Data Storage

items.title: Sensitive data in SharedPreferences / NSUserDefaults
items.description: Tokens, passwords or PII written to SharedPreferences (Android) or NSUserDefaults (iOS) are stored as cleartext XML/plist and are trivially readable on a rooted/jailbroken device or via backup.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect these plaintext stores:

```
adb shell run-as <pkg> cat /data/data/<pkg>/shared_prefs/*.xml
objection -g <bundle-id> explore   # ios nsuserdefaults
```
items.how_to_fix: Never store secrets/PII in SharedPreferences/NSUserDefaults; use Keystore/Keychain-backed encryption (`EncryptedSharedPreferences`, Keychain), and minimize persistence. Re-inspect.

items.title: Unencrypted SQLite / Realm databases
items.description: App databases holding credentials, session data or PII are stored without encryption (no SQLCipher / Realm encryption), exposing all rows on a compromised device.
items.how_to_find:
Pull and open app databases:

```
adb shell run-as <pkg> ls /data/data/<pkg>/databases/
sqlite3 app.db .dump | grep -iE 'token|password|card'
```
items.how_to_fix: Encrypt databases (SQLCipher / encrypted Realm) with a Keystore/Keychain-protected key, and store as little sensitive data as possible. Re-check the DB is encrypted.

items.title: Sensitive data on external/shared storage
items.description: Writing sensitive files to external storage (Android getExternalFilesDir/SD card or world-readable paths) exposes them to other apps and removes OS sandbox protection.
items.how_to_find:
Check world-readable/external storage:

```
adb shell ls -R /sdcard/Android/data/<pkg>/
```
items.how_to_fix: Keep sensitive files in app-private storage (encrypted), avoid external/shared storage for secrets, and use scoped storage. Re-check external storage is clean.

items.title: Sensitive data captured in device backups
items.description: Android auto-backup (allowBackup) and iOS iCloud/iTunes backups can copy app data off-device.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether backups capture secrets:

```
adb backup -f data.ab <pkg> && ( printf ... ) # unpack and inspect
```
items.how_to_fix: Exclude sensitive data from backups (`allowBackup=false` / `dataExtractionRules`; iOS set `isExcludedFromBackup` / Keychain `ThisDeviceOnly`). Re-test backup contents.

items.title: Sensitive data leaked to logs
items.description: Logcat / NSLog / os_log statements that print tokens, passwords or PII leak sensitive data to system logs, crash reports and any app holding the READ_LOGS capability on older devices.
items.how_to_find:
Watch logs during use:

```
adb logcat | grep -iE 'token|password|card|ssn'
```
items.how_to_fix: Strip sensitive data from logs, disable debug logging in release, and use redaction. Re-check logcat/syslog.

items.title: Sensitive data exposed via clipboard
items.description: Allowing copy of passwords/OTPs/tokens to the clipboard exposes them to any app reading the pasteboard; iOS universal clipboard can sync them across devices.
items.how_to_find:
Monitor clipboard while using sensitive fields:

```
objection -g <pkg> explore   # android clipboard monitor
```
items.how_to_fix: Avoid auto-copying secrets; mark sensitive fields to exclude from clipboard where possible, and clear/expire clipboard after use. Re-test.

items.title: Sensitive data in screenshot / UI cache
items.description: The OS snapshots the app on backgrounding for the task switcher.
items.how_to_find: Check the app-switcher snapshot and UI cache for sensitive screens.
items.how_to_fix: Mask sensitive screens on backgrounding (Android `FLAG_SECURE`; iOS overlay/blur), and avoid caching sensitive UI. Re-test the snapshot.

sections.title: Data Storage & Privacy — Keychain / Keystore Usage

items.title: Secrets not stored in Keychain / Keystore
items.description: Cryptographic keys and credentials kept in plain files or preferences instead of the OS secure store lose hardware isolation and OS access controls.
items.how_to_find:
Check where secrets/keys live (files/prefs vs Keystore/Keychain):

```
adb shell run-as <pkg> grep -R -iE 'token|key' /data/data/<pkg>/
objection -g <bundle-id> explore   # ios keychain dump
```
items.how_to_fix: Store all secrets/keys in Android Keystore / iOS Keychain (hardware-backed where available), not app files. Re-audit secret storage.

items.title: Weak Keychain protection class
items.description: Keychain items using kSecAttrAccessibleAlways or AfterFirstUnlock (with ThisDeviceOnly omitted) remain readable when the device is locked or migrate via backup, weakening protection.
items.how_to_find:
Review Keychain accessibility attributes:

```
objection -g <bundle-id> explore   # ios keychain dump (shows accessibility)
```
items.how_to_fix: Use `WhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly` (+ access control / biometrics) for sensitive items; avoid `Always`/`AfterFirstUnlock` and iCloud sync. Re-verify protection classes.

items.title: Hardware-backed keystore / StrongBox not used
items.description: Keys generated in software rather than the TEE/Secure Enclave/StrongBox can be extracted on a rooted device, defeating at-rest key protection.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether keys are hardware-backed:

```
# Android: KeyInfo.isInsideSecureHardware() ; request StrongBox
# review key generation params in decompiled code
```
items.how_to_fix: Generate keys in the Keystore with `setIsStrongBoxBacked(true)` where supported (fallback to TEE), and use Secure Enclave on iOS. Re-verify keys are hardware-backed.

items.title: Key material extractable at runtime
items.description: Keys imported into memory or exported from the keystore (rather than used via opaque handles) can be hooked and dumped at runtime with Frida.
items.how_to_find:
Attempt to extract keys via instrumentation:

```
frida-trace -U -i "*Cipher*" -i "*KeyStore*" -f <pkg>
```
items.how_to_fix: Keep keys non-exportable inside Keystore/Secure Enclave (operate on data via the key handle, never export raw key), and add anti-hooking. Re-test extraction fails.

items.title: Keys not bound to user authentication
items.description: High-value keys that do not require user authentication (biometric/PIN) for use can be exercised by malware or an attacker with device access.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether key use requires user auth (`setUserAuthenticationRequired`):

```
# review key generation in decompiled code / KeyGenParameterSpec
```
items.how_to_fix: Bind sensitive keys to user authentication (biometric/device credential) with a sensible validity window, invalidating on biometric enrollment change. Re-verify auth-bound keys.

items.title: Cleartext (HTTP) traffic permitted
items.description: Any plaintext HTTP traffic exposes data to interception and tampering.
items.how_to_find:
Check for cleartext allowances and observe traffic:

```
grep -i 'usesCleartextTraffic' out/AndroidManifest.xml
# review res/xml/network_security_config.xml ; iOS: NSAllowsArbitraryLoads
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable cleartext (`usesCleartextTraffic=false` / `cleartextTrafficPermitted=false`; remove ATS exceptions), require HTTPS everywhere, and enforce HSTS server-side. Re-test no HTTP is used.

items.title: Disabled or broken TLS certificate validation
items.description: Trust-all TrustManagers, hostname verifiers that always return true, or ignored TLS errors let an attacker MITM the connection with any certificate.
items.how_to_find:
Look for disabled validation (trust-all TrustManagers/HostnameVerifiers):

```
grep -REn 'checkServerTrusted|ALLOW_ALL_HOSTNAME|TrustAllCerts|X509TrustManager' out/
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove trust-all/allow-all-hostname code, use default platform validation (+ pinning), and fail closed on validation errors. Re-scan and test with an invalid cert.

items.title: Certificate / public-key pinning present
items.description: Pinning the server certificate or public key raises the bar against MITM by rejecting CAs not in the pin set.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether pinning is implemented:

```
grep -REn 'CertificatePinner|pin-set|TrustKit' out/
```

No pinning on sensitive apps is a gap.
items.how_to_fix: Implement pinning (OkHttp `CertificatePinner` / Network Security Config `<pin-set>` / TrustKit) with backup pins + rotation plan, combined with tamper detection. Re-verify pinning is enforced.

items.title: Weak TLS versions or cipher suites accepted
items.description: Allowing TLS 1.0/1.1 or weak ciphers exposes traffic to downgrade and cryptographic attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Test the backend's TLS config the app connects to:

```
sslscan <api-host>:443
testssl.sh <api-host>
```
items.how_to_fix: Require TLS 1.2+ (prefer 1.3), disable weak ciphers/protocols server-side, and configure the client for modern TLS only. Re-scan to confirm weak options are gone.

items.title: iOS App Transport Security misconfiguration
items.description: NSAllowsArbitraryLoads or broad NSExceptionDomains in Info.plist disable ATS protections and allow insecure connections.
items.how_to_find:
Review ATS settings in Info.plist:

```
plutil -p ipa/Payload/*.app/Info.plist | grep -iA10 'NSAppTransportSecurity'
```

`NSAllowsArbitraryLoads=true` or broad exceptions = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove `NSAllowsArbitraryLoads` and per-domain exceptions, require TLS 1.2+ with forward secrecy, and justify any minimal exception. Re-review the plist.

sections.title: Network & Transport — Cert Pinning & Interception Resistance

items.title: Pinning bypassable via Frida / Objection
items.description: Pinning implemented purely in app code can be neutralised with universal Frida/Objection scripts, allowing full traffic interception on a rooted/jailbroken device.
items.how_to_find:
Test how easily pinning falls:

```
objection -g <pkg> explore   # android sslpinning disable
frida -U -f <pkg> -l frida-multiple-unpinning.js
```
items.how_to_fix: Add anti-Frida/anti-hooking + integrity checks alongside pinning, obfuscate, and enforce all authz server-side so a bypass doesn't grant access. Re-test resistance.

items.title: Weak Android network_security_config
items.description: A network security config that trusts user certificates, permits cleartext for some domains, or omits pin-sets weakens transport protection.
items.how_to_find:
Review the network security config:

```
cat out/res/xml/network_security_config.xml
# look for cleartextTrafficPermitted=true, trust-anchors user, debug-overrides
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `cleartextTrafficPermitted=false`, trust only system CAs (no `user` trust anchors in release), add `<pin-set>`, and scope per-domain. Re-review the config.

items.title: App trusts user-installed CA certificates
items.description: Trusting the user CA store (Android default below API 24, or explicitly via config) lets a proxy CA intercept traffic without rooting.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether user CAs are trusted (enables easy MITM):

```
# network_security_config <trust-anchors><certificates src="user"/>
```
items.how_to_fix: Trust only system CAs in release builds (remove `user` trust anchors), and add pinning. Re-test that a user-installed CA can't intercept.

items.title: Pinning removable via repackaging / binary patching
items.description: When pinning lives only in the app, an attacker can patch the smali/binary and resign the app to strip it, then analyse traffic at scale.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether pinning survives repackaging:

```
apktool d app.apk ; # patch smali/config ; apktool b ; sign & install
```
items.how_to_fix: Add integrity/anti-tamper checks (signature verification, Play Integrity/App Attest server-side), obfuscate, and enforce server-side authz. Re-test a repackaged build is detected/rejected.

items.title: Client-side local authentication bypass
items.description: When the login/PIN decision is made only on the device, hooking the check or patching the binary lets an attacker bypass authentication entirely.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether login/authorization decisions made on-device can be bypassed:

```
objection -g <pkg> explore   # patch auth checks
frida -U -f <pkg> -l bypass.js
```
items.how_to_fix: Make all authentication/authorization decisions server-side; treat client checks as UX only. Re-test that bypassing client checks grants no access.

items.title: Biometric authentication bypass
items.description: Event-only biometric callbacks (a boolean success) rather than crypto-bound BiometricPrompt/LAContext can be hooked to return success without a real biometric match.
items.how_to_find:
Test biometric flows for event-only (bypassable) vs crypto-bound implementations:

```
objection -g <pkg> explore   # ios ui biometrics_bypass
frida -U -f <pkg> -l biometric-bypass.js
```
items.how_to_fix: Use crypto-bound biometrics (BiometricPrompt with a CryptoObject / Keychain access control), so a spoofed success callback yields no usable key. Re-test bypass fails.

items.title: Insecure session token storage
items.description: Session/refresh tokens stored in cleartext preferences or files (instead of Keychain/Keystore) can be stolen from a compromised device or backup.
items.how_to_find:
Find where session tokens are stored:

```
adb shell run-as <pkg> grep -R -iE 'token|jwt|session' /data/data/<pkg>/
```
items.how_to_fix: Store tokens in Keystore/Keychain-backed encrypted storage, use short-lived tokens + refresh, and clear on logout. Re-inspect storage.

items.title: Session handling inside WebViews
items.description: Session cookies/tokens exposed to embedded WebViews can leak via the JS bridge, cookie store, or be hijacked if the WebView loads untrusted content.
items.how_to_find:
Check how WebViews handle session cookies/tokens (shared cookie store, JS access):

```
grep -REn 'CookieManager|WKWebsiteDataStore|evaluateJavascript' out/
```
items.how_to_fix: Isolate WebView session state, use HttpOnly/Secure cookies, avoid exposing tokens to JS, and clear WebView data on logout. Re-test.

items.title: Trusting offline / cached authentication state
items.description: Apps that grant access based on cached credentials or entitlements without periodic server revalidation can be abused after a credential or entitlement is revoked.
items.how_to_find: Test offline behavior — does the app grant access from cached auth without server validation?
items.how_to_fix: Re-validate auth with the server for sensitive actions, expire cached state, and don't trust offline flags for authorization. Re-test offline access.

items.title: Session not invalidated server-side on logout
items.description: If logout only clears local state while the server-side token stays valid, a stolen token remains usable, defeating the logout control.
items.how_to_find:
After logout, replay the old token:

```
# capture token, logout, then replay the request with the old token
```
items.how_to_fix: Invalidate/revoke tokens server-side on logout (and on password change), use short TTLs, and maintain a revocation list. Re-test the old token is rejected.

items.title: Hardcoded encryption keys
items.description: Symmetric keys, IVs or passphrases embedded in the binary/resources can be recovered by any analyst, rendering the encryption useless.
items.how_to_find:
Search for embedded keys/IVs:

```
grep -REn 'SecretKeySpec|IvParameterSpec|"[A-Fa-f0-9]{32,}"' out/
strings -a <bin> | grep -iE 'key|iv'
```
items.how_to_fix: Never hardcode keys; derive per-user/per-device keys in Keystore/Secure Enclave or provision from the server, and rotate exposed keys. Re-scan.

items.title: Weak or broken cryptographic algorithms
items.description: Use of DES/3DES, RC4, ECB mode, or MD5/SHA-1 for security purposes provides little real protection and is exploitable.
items.how_to_find:
Find weak crypto (MD5/SHA1/DES/ECB):

```
grep -REn 'MD5|SHA-?1|DES|RC4|ECB|Cipher.getInstance\("AES"\)' out/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use modern primitives (AES-GCM, SHA-256+, bcrypt/argon2 for passwords), avoid ECB, and use vetted libraries. Re-scan for weak algorithms.

items.title: Insecure random number generation
items.description: Using java.util.Random / non-CSPRNG sources for keys, IVs, tokens or salts produces predictable values that undermine cryptographic security.
items.how_to_find:
Find non-crypto RNG used for security values:

```
grep -REn 'new Random\(|Math.random|arc4random\(\)' out/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use `SecureRandom` (Android) / `SecRandomCopyBytes` (iOS) for tokens/IVs/salts. Re-scan.

items.title: Custom / home-grown cryptography
items.description: Roll-your-own encryption, XOR obfuscation or proprietary schemes almost always contain flaws and should be replaced with vetted primitives.
items.how_to_find:
Look for bespoke encryption/encoding used as crypto:

```
grep -REn 'xor|custom.?crypt|encrypt\(' out/
```
items.how_to_fix: Replace home-grown crypto with standard, vetted libraries and protocols (never roll your own). Re-review crypto usage.

items.title: Static IV / nonce reuse
items.description: Reusing a fixed IV/nonce with the same key (especially with CTR/GCM) breaks confidentiality and, for GCM, can leak the authentication key.
items.how_to_find:
Find fixed IVs/nonces:

```
grep -REn 'IvParameterSpec\(|GCMParameterSpec\(' out/   # trace the IV source
```
items.how_to_fix: Use a fresh random IV/nonce per encryption (never reuse with the same key, critical for GCM/CTR), and store/transmit it with the ciphertext. Re-verify IVs are random per-message.

items.title: Unprotected exported components
items.description: Exported activities, services, broadcast receivers and content providers without permission checks let other apps invoke privileged functionality.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate exported components callable by other apps:

```
grep -B2 -A6 'exported="true"' out/AndroidManifest.xml
drozer  # run app.package.attacksurface <pkg>
```
items.how_to_fix: Unexport internal components, add signature-level permissions + input validation to public ones, and avoid exposing sensitive actions. Re-audit.

items.title: Insecure / exported content providers
items.description: Exported content providers or those vulnerable to path traversal / SQL injection in their query interface can leak app data to other apps.
items.how_to_find:
Query exported providers (SQLi / path traversal / data exposure):

```
adb shell content query --uri content://<authority>/<path>
drozer  # app.provider.query / finduri / traversal
```
items.how_to_fix: Unexport providers not meant for sharing, enforce permissions + path/URI validation, use parameterized queries, and restrict grant-URI permissions. Re-test provider access.

items.title: Deep link / URL scheme hijacking
items.description: Custom URL schemes can be claimed by multiple apps; unverified app links/universal links can be hijacked, enabling redirection, data theft or auth-code interception.
items.how_to_find:
Test custom schemes (any app can register the same scheme):

```
adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d "myapp://sensitive?..." <pkg>
```
items.how_to_fix: Prefer verified App Links / Universal Links over custom schemes, validate all params, require auth for sensitive actions, and don't pass secrets in links. Re-test hijacking.

items.title: Unvalidated deep link parameters
items.description: Deep links that pass parameters straight into navigation, WebView loads or sensitive actions enable open redirect, parameter tampering or unauthorized actions.
items.how_to_find:
Fuzz deep-link params into sinks (WebView, file, SQL, redirects):

```
adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d "myapp://open?url=https://evil" <pkg>
```
items.how_to_fix: Validate/allowlist deep-link parameters, never load attacker-controlled URLs into WebViews or use params in file/SQL sinks, and canonicalize inputs. Re-test.

items.title: Intent redirection / confused deputy
items.description: A component that forwards an attacker-supplied nested Intent (startActivity on an extra) can be abused to launch internal components or leak data.
items.how_to_find:
Look for components that forward an attacker-supplied Intent (nested intent extras):

```
grep -REn 'getParcelableExtra\(.*Intent|startActivity\(.*getIntent' out/
```
items.how_to_fix: Don't forward untrusted Intents from exported components; validate the target/action, use explicit intents, and restrict who can trigger the redirect. Re-test.

items.title: Sensitive data shared via pasteboard / IPC extras
items.description: Passing secrets through the global pasteboard or broadcast intents exposes them to other apps that monitor those channels.
items.how_to_find: Check for secrets passed in Intent extras / pasteboard between components/apps.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid putting secrets in Intent extras/pasteboard; use secure, permissioned IPC and clear/expire shared data. Re-test.

items.title: Tapjacking and overlay attacks
items.description: Sensitive Android screens that do not filter touches obscured by overlays can be tapjacked: a malicious app draws over the UI and tricks the user into approving actions or leaking input.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether sensitive screens can be overlaid (tapjacking):

```
# overlay a transparent window over the app's sensitive buttons
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `filterTouchesWhenObscured=true` / `setFilterTouchesWhenObscured` on sensitive views, detect overlays, and avoid critical one-tap actions. Re-test overlay is blocked.

items.title: Insecure JavaScript bridge (addJavascriptInterface)
items.description: Exposing native objects to web content via addJavascriptInterface / WKScriptMessageHandler can give untrusted JS access to native capabilities and, historically, RCE.
items.how_to_find:
Find JS bridges exposed to web content:

```
grep -REn 'addJavascriptInterface|@JavascriptInterface|WKScriptMessageHandler' out/
```

Bridge + untrusted content = RCE-class (critical).
items.how_to_fix: Only expose JS bridges to trusted, first-party content over HTTPS; annotate minimal `@JavascriptInterface` methods, validate all input, and avoid loading untrusted URLs. Re-audit bridges.

items.title: WebView file:// and content access enabled
items.description: Enabling setAllowFileAccess / AllowFileAccessFromFileURLs / AllowUniversalAccessFromFileURLs lets web content read local files, enabling cross-zone data theft.
items.how_to_find:
Check risky WebView settings:

```
grep -REn 'setAllowFileAccess|setAllowUniversalAccessFromFileURLs|setAllowContentAccess' out/
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable file/content/universal access from file URLs (`setAllowFileAccessFromFileURLs(false)`, etc.) unless required, and don't load untrusted content with these enabled. Re-audit settings.

items.title: WebView loads untrusted / attacker-controlled URLs
items.description: Calling loadUrl/loadData with input derived from intents, deep links or server responses can load malicious content into a privileged WebView.
items.how_to_find:
Trace URL sources loaded into WebViews (deep links, server data):

```
grep -REn 'loadUrl\(|loadData\(|load\(URLRequest' out/
```
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist WebView URLs to trusted domains over HTTPS, validate any dynamic URL, and never load user/attacker-controlled URLs (esp. with a JS bridge). Re-test.

items.title: WebView mixed content allowed
items.description: Setting MIXED_CONTENT_ALWAYS_ALLOW lets an HTTPS page load HTTP subresources, enabling MITM injection into the WebView.
items.how_to_find:
Check mixed-content mode:

```
grep -REn 'setMixedContentMode|MIXED_CONTENT_ALWAYS_ALLOW' out/
```
items.how_to_fix: Set `MIXED_CONTENT_NEVER_ALLOW`, require HTTPS for all WebView resources, and remove cleartext. Re-audit.

items.title: WKWebView / UIWebView misconfiguration
items.description: Use of deprecated UIWebView, enabling JavaScript where unneeded, or message handlers exposed to untrusted origins weakens the iOS WebView surface.
items.how_to_find:
Check for deprecated UIWebView and insecure WKWebView config:

```
grep -REn 'UIWebView|WKWebView|javaScriptEnabled|allowsArbitrary' out/ ipa/
```
items.how_to_fix: Migrate off UIWebView to WKWebView, disable JS where not needed, restrict navigation to trusted origins, and validate message-handler input. Re-audit.

sections.title: Code Quality & Resilience — Reverse Engineering & Resilience

items.title: No code obfuscation
items.description: Unobfuscated code with original class/method names makes reverse engineering, logic recovery and bypass development trivial for high-value apps.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether release code is obfuscated:

```
jadx-gui app.apk   # readable class/method names => not obfuscated
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable R8/ProGuard (Android) / obfuscation (iOS) for release, strip symbols, and obfuscate sensitive logic. Note: obfuscation is defense-in-depth, not a substitute for server-side controls. Re-check the release build.

items.title: App shipped as debuggable
items.description: A release build with android:debuggable=true (or get-task-allow on iOS) lets anyone attach a debugger, dump memory and inspect runtime state.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm the release build isn't debuggable:

```
aapt dump badging app.apk | grep -i debuggable
```
items.how_to_fix: Ensure `debuggable=false` in release, gate on CI, and strip debug code/logging. Re-verify.

items.title: Missing root / jailbreak detection
items.description: Absent root/jailbreak detection means the app runs on fully compromised devices where storage, memory and pinning protections are void, with no resistance signal.
items.how_to_find: Test on a rooted/jailbroken device — does the app run unrestricted?
items.how_to_fix: Add layered root/JB detection + attestation (Play Integrity / App Attest) validated server-side, and restrict sensitive functionality on compromised devices. Re-test.

items.title: Missing integrity / anti-tamper checks
items.description: Without signature/integrity verification, an attacker can repackage and resign the app (strip pinning, inject code) and distribute a modified build.
items.how_to_find:
Repackage and resign the app — does it still run?

```
apktool d app.apk ; apktool b ; sign ; install
```
items.how_to_fix: Add signature/integrity verification + server-side attestation so tampered/repackaged builds are detected and blocked. Re-test a modified build.

items.title: Missing Frida / emulator / hooking detection
items.description: No detection of dynamic instrumentation (Frida/Xposed) or emulators allows full runtime manipulation of the app, defeating client-side controls.
items.how_to_find:
Run under Frida / on an emulator — is it detected?

```
frida -U -f <pkg> -l detect.js
```
items.how_to_fix: Add anti-Frida/anti-hooking + emulator detection (defense-in-depth), obfuscate the checks, and keep trust decisions server-side. Re-test.

items.title: Hardcoded API keys / secrets
items.description: API keys, tokens and signing secrets baked into the binary, strings.xml or resources are recoverable from the package and can be abused against backend services.
items.how_to_find:
Scan the app for API keys/secrets:

```
grep -REn 'api[_-]?key|secret|AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}|AIza[0-9A-Za-z_-]{35}' out/ ipa/
strings -a <bin> | grep -iE 'key|secret|token'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove all shipped secrets (extractable), move them server-side behind authenticated APIs, use short-lived tokens, and rotate exposed keys. Re-scan.

items.title: Embedded credentials / accounts
items.description: Hardcoded usernames, passwords or service-account credentials grant anyone who extracts them direct access to backend or third-party systems.
items.how_to_find:
Search for embedded usernames/passwords:

```
grep -REn 'password|passwd|username|-----BEGIN' out/ ipa/
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove embedded credentials, use per-user auth against the server, and rotate anything exposed. Re-scan.

items.title: Exposed Firebase / cloud configuration
items.description: Firebase config, cloud bucket URLs and tokens in the app can reveal open databases/buckets or misconfigured rules, leading to data exposure.
items.how_to_find:
Find and test Firebase/cloud config (e.g. open Realtime DB):

```
grep -REn 'firebaseio.com|google-services.json|GoogleService-Info' out/ ipa/
curl -s 'https://<project>.firebaseio.com/.json'   # open DB check
```
items.how_to_fix: Lock down Firebase security rules (no public read/write), restrict API-key usage, and move sensitive config server-side. Re-test the DB/rules.

items.title: Secrets in strings / resources / assets
items.description: Endpoints, encryption keys and tokens placed in res/values, assets, or BuildConfig fields are easily dumped without even decompiling code.
items.how_to_find:
Search resources/assets:

```
grep -REn 'key|secret|token|password' out/res/ out/assets/
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove secrets from resources/assets, move server-side, and rotate exposed values. Re-scan.

items.title: Secrets hidden in native libraries
items.description: Moving keys/secrets into .so / native code only raises the bar slightly; static analysis of the binary recovers them, so it is not a real protection.
items.how_to_find:
Extract strings from native libs:

```
strings -a out/lib/*/*.so | grep -iE 'key|secret|token|http'
```
items.how_to_fix: Don't rely on native libs to hide secrets (still extractable); move them server-side and rotate. Re-scan the .so files.
